clues: The disease was first reported after Columbus's return from the New World causing rapid devastating skin eruptiosn and rotting flesh, often leading to death within months
Known as 'The Great Imitator', this disease was treated with mercury for centuries
Studying this disease, the Tuskegee Study is still a source of medical mistrust, acting as an example of historical trauma
Introduced in 1988, nearly 30% of hospitalized patients today are prescribed this medication
Available over the counter, this medication irreversibly binds to an ATPase enzyme to block action
While initially considered low risk, long term use of this class of medications has been linked to increased risks of fractures, pneumonia, C. diff, and CKD
During the war in Afghanistan, this was used by the CIA to bribe triabl chiefs for information on Taliban supply routes
It only took two years to get FDA approval in 1998 after trials showed efficacy and 5.3 million prescriptions were written in the first 6 months
Originally used approved for men, this medication is now used for all genders after a second FDA indication was approved in 2005
Initially linked to tuberculosis, the disease is now known primarily as an autoimmune disorder
Due to stimulation of melanin production, a common sign is darkening of the skin, particularly in skin folds and gums
Treatments have advanced from feeding patients raw animal glands in the 1950s to mondern hormone replacement
Despite its name, this medication actually works by binding to calcium channels in the brain

A subsidiary company of Pfizer illegally marketed this medication using fake studies and pushing doctors to prescribe for off-label use without FDA approval leading to a $430 million dollar penalty in 2004
Initially considered to have low misuse potential, abuse of this medication is now an emerging concern especially among those with opioid abuse histories
